# UC-22.51.15 · OT-Tier Phishing and Email-Borne Threat Detection

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Advanced &middot; **Pillar:** Security &middot; **Type:** Compliance, Email, Phishing &middot; **Wave:** Run &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We watch all the emails coming to plant workers. We look out for fake-vendor emails, dangerous attachments, dodgy links, and people quietly reporting trouble. If anything bad lands or someone clicks something they shouldn't have, we ring the bell.*

---

## Description

Filters incoming email events down to OT personnel, evaluates sender authenticity against the OT-vendor allowlist and lookalike-domain patterns, correlates with sandbox verdicts and end-user phishing reports, and classifies the message.

## Value

OT personnel are a high-value target for spear-phishing (Triton/TRISIS, INDUSTROYER2). Generic IT phishing detection often misses OT-specific patterns: typosquats of OT-vendor domains (siemenz.com, rockwel-automation.net), OT-themed lures (a fake firmware update from Schneider), and OT-personnel-targeted spear-phishing with crafted CVE references. This UC raises the floor on those threats by explicitly modeling OT-personnel scope and OT-vendor lookalike patterns.

## Implementation

(1) Onboard the email gateway in use (Defender for Office 365 / Proofpoint / Mimecast). (2) Maintain `ot_personnel.csv` from HRIS, scoped on `ot_scope_flag=true`. (3) Maintain `ot_vendor_domain_allowlist.csv` of canonical OT-vendor sender domains (siemens.com, rockwellautomation.com, schneider-electric.com, etc.). (4) Maintain `ot_vendor_lookalike_patterns.csv` of typosquat patterns with `lookalike_for_vendor` mapping. (5) Onboard PhishAlert / Defender's built-in user-reported phishing channel. (6) Schedule UC-22.51.15 every 15 minutes.

## Evidence

Saved search `UC-22.51.15` archived every 15 minutes to `audit_evidence` with `otcc_clause="OTCC-2-12-3-1"`. Each row preserves (recipient_email, recipient_role, sender_address, sender_domain, subject_summary, attachment_count, attachment_hashes, has_ot_vendor_lookalike, sandbox_verdict, url_count, suspicious_url_count, reported_by_user, click_observed, classification, observed_at). Dashboard `NCA OTCC – OT Phishing` shows incoming-threat volume to OT personnel and the click-or-quarantine outcomes.

## Control test

### Positive scenario

Send a test email from `update@siemenz-support.com` (typosquat of `siemens.com`) to a test OT engineer mailbox with a malicious-pattern attachment (EICAR string in a .docm file). The email gateway sandbox should classify as `malicious`; UC-22.51.15 MUST surface the event with `sandbox_verdict="malicious"` and `has_ot_vendor_lookalike=true`, route an alert to SOC, and archive.

### Negative scenario

A legitimate vendor email from `psirt@siemens.com` to the same recipient, with no attachment and only the vendor-portal URL, MUST NOT be flagged.

## Detailed Implementation

## Prerequisites

- Splunk Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud.
- The email gateway in use (one or more):
  - Microsoft Defender for Office 365 — via the Microsoft Graph Security API modular input.
  - Proofpoint Email Protection — Splunk Add-on for Proofpoint (Splunkbase 5024).
  - Mimecast Email Security — Splunk Add-on for Mimecast (Splunkbase 5283).
- PhishAlert / reported-phishing feed integrated:
  - Microsoft 'Report Message' add-in writes to AzureGraph; pulled via Defender for Office 365.
  - Cofense PhishMe Reporter writes to Cofense Triage; pulled via REST modular input into `index=email`.
  - KnowBe4 PhishER — REST API integration.
- `index=email` (24-month retention), `index=audit_evidence` (already provisioned).
- Lookups:
  - `ot_personnel.csv` — owned by HR-business-partner-to-cyber. Columns: `email`, `user_id`, `role`, `business_unit`, `ot_scope_flag`. Refreshed daily.
  - `ot_vendor_domain_allowlist.csv` — owned by OT cybersecurity engineering. Columns: `sender_domain`, `vendor`, `purpose`, `allowlist_owner`. Reviewed annually.
  - `ot_vendor_lookalike_patterns.csv` — owned by OT cybersecurity engineering. Columns: `sender_domain`, `lookalike_for_vendor`, `first_seen`, `reference`. Reviewed quarterly. Examples:

    ```csv
    sender_domain,lookalike_for_vendor,first_seen,reference
    siemenz.com,siemens,2024-01-15,internal-IOC-2024-001
    siemenz-support.com,siemens,2024-03-02,internal-IOC-2024-007
    rockwel-automation.net,rockwell,2024-02-19,internal-IOC-2024-004
    schneidre-electric.com,schneider,2024-04-10,public-OSINT
    ```

  - `phish_simulation_campaigns.csv` — owned by OT cybersecurity awareness team. Columns: `campaign_id`, `sender_pattern`, `start_date`, `end_date`.
  - KV Store `otcc_22_51_15_exceptions` and `otcc_22_51_15_attachment_whitelist`.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Defender for Office 365 via the Microsoft Graph Security API:

```ini
[microsoft_graph_security://email_alerts]
client_id = <aad-app-id>
tenant = <tenant>
client_secret = <key-vault-reference>
resource = email_threats
index = email
sourcetype = ms-defender-365:email
interval = 300
```

Proofpoint via SIEM API:

```ini
[proofpoint://email_protection]
user = <api-user>
pass = <api-password-key-vault>
endpoint = https://tap-api-v2.proofpoint.com/v2
index = email
sourcetype = proofpoint:email
interval = 300
```

Mimecast via API:

```ini
[mimecast://email_security]
app_id = <mimecast-app-id>
app_key = <mimecast-app-key-vault>
access_key = <mimecast-access-key-vault>
secret_key = <mimecast-secret-key-vault>
endpoint = https://us-api.mimecast.com
index = email
sourcetype = mimecast:email
interval = 300
```

Validate:

```spl
index=email earliest=-1d
| stats count by sourcetype
```

Volume guidance: depends heavily on email volume. For an estate with 200 OT-scoped recipients, expect 1k–10k events/day; license <100 MB/day.

## Step 2 — Create the search and alert

The search filters to OT-personnel recipients first, then enriches with allowlist and lookalike-pattern lookups, then computes the final classification. The classification ordering is intentional: `malicious` sandbox verdict trumps everything, then `vendor lookalike`, then `suspicious URL`, then `general suspicious`, then `user reported`. The `reported_by_user=true` outcome is preserved even on otherwise-clean messages because user reporting is itself an evidence event of the control's effectiveness.

**Schedule**:

```ini
[UC-22.51.15: OT-Tier Phishing and Email-Borne Threat Detection]
cron_schedule = */15 * * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -15m@m
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index, notable, email
action.summary_index._name = audit_evidence
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.51.15,reg=NCA_OTCC,clause=OTCC-2-12-3-1
action.notable.param.rule_title = NCA OTCC OT-tier phishing detected
action.notable.param.security_domain = network
action.notable.param.severity = high
action.email.to = ot-soc@example.com
alert.severity = 4
```

Notable creation on `classification IN ("malicious","suspicious_vendor_lookalike")` only; the other classifications route via email + audit_evidence.

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Email-feed health**:

```spl
index=email earliest=-1d
| stats count by sourcetype
```

Zero from any onboarded gateway means the modular input is broken.

**(b) OT-personnel filter health**:

```spl
| inputlookup ot_personnel.csv
| where ot_scope_flag="true"
| stats count
```

Review the count weekly; should match the OT-personnel headcount within HR's reporting.

**(c) Lookalike-pattern review**:

```spl
| inputlookup ot_vendor_lookalike_patterns.csv
| eval age_days = round((now() - strptime(first_seen, "%Y-%m-%d")) / 86400)
| stats avg(age_days) AS avg_age max(age_days) AS max_age count
```

Quarterly review by OT cybersecurity engineering; remove patterns >2 years old that have shown no recent activity.

**(d) Positive control**: per `controlTest.positiveScenario`. Confirm notable within 15 minutes.

**(e) Negative control**: per `controlTest.negativeScenario`.

## Step 4 — Operationalize

**Dashboard**: `NCA OTCC – OT Phishing`.

- **Row 1, single values**: 'Malicious to OT personnel 24h', 'Vendor lookalike 24h', 'Clicked-after-delivery 24h', 'User-reported 24h'.
- **Row 2, sortable table**: every flagged email with drill-through to the gateway's investigation view.
- **Row 3, top-N recipients**: OT personnel most-targeted in the last 30 days.
- **Row 4, vendor-lookalike funnel**: visualises delivered → sandboxed → clicked.
- **Row 5, 13-month trend by classification**.

**Runbook for `classification="malicious"`** (owner: OT SOC):

1. **Within 15 minutes**: verify whether the recipient has clicked or opened the attachment via Defender / Proofpoint trace.
2. **If clicked or attachment-opened**: treat as a P1 OT cybersecurity incident. Isolate the recipient's endpoint. Engage IR per UC-22.51.16.
3. **If only delivered, not clicked**: instruct the email gateway to remove the message from all OT-personnel mailboxes (zap, in Defender; recall, in Mimecast). Block the sender domain. Add to `ot_vendor_lookalike_patterns.csv` if a vendor lookalike.
4. **Document** in the OT-cybersecurity incident ticket.

**Runbook for `classification="suspicious_vendor_lookalike"`**:

1. **Within 1 hour**: trace whether the message was opened.
2. Block the sender at the gateway and add to the lookalike pattern lookup.
3. Send a targeted notification to OT personnel reminding them of the canonical vendor domain.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting

**Defender for Office 365 alerts arrive 15–60 minutes late** — the Graph Security API has known latency on certain alert types. Use the EOP `MessageTrace` API in addition for fresher data.

**`sandbox_verdict` is always null** — the gateway is not configured for sandbox scanning of attachments. Enable Safe Attachments (Defender), TAP (Proofpoint), or Attachment Protect (Mimecast) for OT-scoped recipients.

**`reported_by_user` events appear with hours of latency** — the PhishAlert add-in's queue ingestion is hourly by default. For faster signals, integrate the gateway's direct user-reporting feed (Defender Submissions, Mimecast PhishAlert).

**OT-specific failure modes**:

- **Vendor PSIRT bulk lists**: large OEMs send PSIRT bulletins from bulk senders (e.g. `notify@siemens.com`). These may be flagged by aggressive lookalike rules; whitelist explicitly.
- **Internal OT-engineering distribution lists**: messages sent to large internal DLs may exceed the per-recipient join cost. Pre-aggregate per `message_id` and broadcast against the DL membership at search time.
- **Spearphishing through technical-conference replies**: an OT engineer's email thread reply on a topical conference list can have spoofed reply-to addresses. Cross-reference DMARC/DKIM/SPF status in the search; messages with DMARC fail PLUS lookalike domain are the highest-risk combination.
- **Encrypted attachments (S/MIME, password-protected ZIPs)**: sandbox cannot inspect, but the protected attachment is itself suspicious from an OT vendor. Treat as `suspicious` if S/MIME from a sender not on the allowlist.

## SPL

```spl
index=email sourcetype IN ("ms-defender-365:email","proofpoint:email","mimecast:email") earliest=-15m@m
| eval recipient_email=coalesce(recipient_email, to, recipient)
| lookup ot_personnel.csv email AS recipient_email OUTPUT user_id role business_unit ot_scope_flag
| where ot_scope_flag="true"
| eval sender_address=coalesce(sender_address, from)
| eval sender_domain=lower(replace(sender_address, "^[^@]+@", ""))
| eval subject_summary=substr(subject, 0, 120)
| eval observed_at=_time
| eval attachment_count = coalesce(attachment_count, 0)
| eval url_count = coalesce(url_count, 0)
| lookup ot_vendor_domain_allowlist.csv sender_domain OUTPUT allowlisted_flag
| eval allowlisted_flag = coalesce(allowlisted_flag, "false")
| lookup ot_vendor_lookalike_patterns.csv sender_domain OUTPUT lookalike_for_vendor
| eval has_ot_vendor_lookalike = if(isnotnull(lookalike_for_vendor) AND allowlisted_flag="false", "true", "false")
| eval sandbox_verdict = coalesce(verdict, sandbox_verdict, action)
| eval suspicious_url_count = coalesce(suspicious_url_count, 0)
| join recipient_email observed_at type=left [search index=email sourcetype="*:url:click" earliest=-1h | dedup recipient_email click_time message_id | eval click_observed="true" | table recipient_email message_id click_observed]
| eval click_observed = coalesce(click_observed, "false")
| join sender_address recipient_email observed_at type=left [search index=email action="user_reported_phishing" earliest=-1h | eval reported_by_user="true" | table sender_address recipient_email reported_by_user]
| eval reported_by_user = coalesce(reported_by_user, "false")
| eval classification = case(
    sandbox_verdict="malicious", "malicious",
    has_ot_vendor_lookalike="true", "suspicious_vendor_lookalike",
    suspicious_url_count > 0 AND allowlisted_flag="false", "suspicious_url",
    sandbox_verdict="suspicious", "suspicious",
    reported_by_user="true", "user_reported",
    1=1, "clean")
| where classification!="clean"
| table observed_at recipient_email role business_unit sender_address sender_domain subject_summary attachment_count url_count suspicious_url_count has_ot_vendor_lookalike sandbox_verdict click_observed reported_by_user classification
| sort - observed_at
```

## Visualization

(Panel 1) Single-value 'Malicious to OT personnel 24h', 'Vendor lookalike 24h', 'Clicks on suspicious 24h'. (Panel 2) Top-N OT recipients by suspicious-email count. (Panel 3) Sortable table of flagged messages. (Panel 4) Vendor-lookalike funnel: delivered vs sandboxed vs clicked vs reported. (Panel 5) 13-month trend by classification.

## Known False Positives

1. **Vendor consultants using personal email** — a Rockwell consultant working from a personal Gmail address sends a legitimate procurement message. Suppression: pre-clear known consultants in `ot_vendor_domain_allowlist.csv` for time-bound engagements.

2. **OT cybersecurity awareness programme simulations** — the OT cybersecurity awareness team sends simulated phishing exercises monthly. Suppression: simulations carry `X-PhishSim-Campaign:` header recorded in `phish_simulation_campaigns.csv`; matching campaigns are explicitly excluded from notable creation but still archived for training-effectiveness reporting.

3. **OEM mailing lists with bulk addresses** — OEM PSIRT notifications often originate from `noreply@vendor-bulk.cz`-style domains. Suppression: explicitly allowlist OEM PSIRT bulk-domains in `ot_vendor_domain_allowlist.csv` with `purpose=psirt_bulk` to make the allowlist auditable.

4. **Email gateway sandbox false-positive on benign OT-config files** — some sandboxes flag ladder-logic PDF exports as suspicious because of embedded scripts. Suppression: KV Store `otcc_22_51_15_attachment_whitelist` keyed on attachment hash with the verifying vendor reference.

**Suppression mechanism**: KV Store `otcc_22_51_15_exceptions` keyed on `(sender_address, recipient_email)` with `expires_at`, `reason`, `approved_by`.

## References

- [NCA OTCC — control list portal](https://nca.gov.sa/en/regulatory-documents/controls-list/3)
- [MITRE ATT&CK ICS T0865 — Spearphishing Attachment](https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T0865/)
- [Splunk CIM: Email data model](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/CIM/latest/User/Email)
